backlog.title: Per-repo run lock (FR52)
backlog.description: Implement FR52 — a per-repo advisory flock-based run lock that prevents two concurrent stagehand commit-producing processes from racing on the same repository's HEAD. This is a self-contained concurrency hardening layer composed over the already-implemented v2.0/v3/v2.1 core. The lock is the first line of defense (prevents the common local accidental double-run); the existing §13.5 CAS update-ref is the second (catches cross-host / shared-filesystem races the lock cannot cover). Both stay — defense in depth. One new package (internal/lock), one new exit code (Busy=5), one new third-party dependency (golang.org/x/sys for unix.Flock).

milestones.title: The run lock and its contention behavior
milestones.description: Implement the complete FR52 feature: the internal/lock package with flock-based acquire/release and location resolution, the Busy exit code, the no-op fast path and busy refusal contention behaviors, the snapshot= field wiring into generate.go and decompose.go, and the changeset-level documentation sweep.

tasks.title: Lock primitive (internal/lock + golang.org/x/sys)
tasks.description: Create the self-contained internal/lock package: the flock-based Locker with platform split (unix/windows), the lockDir() XDG location resolver, sha256 canonical-path hashing, lock-file contents writer/reader, and the package-level SetSnapshot() singleton that lets the library publish its frozen tree SHA to the lock file. Unit-tested against temp files (real flock contention, dir resolution, contents round-trip, auto-release).

subtasks.title: Core lock package: Locker struct, flock acquire/release, dir resolver, hash, contents, SetSnapshot singleton, platform split
subtasks.context_scope:
CONTRACT DEFINITION:
1. RESEARCH NOTE: See plan/006_c23e6f286ae7/architecture/system_context.md §1 (lock design) and integration_seams.md §5–§6. The lock is an advisory flock(2) with LOCK_EX|LOCK_NB, auto-released on process death (no stale locks). Location: $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/stagehand/locks/<hash>.lock → else $XDG_CACHE_HOME/... → else ~/.cache/stagehand/locks/<hash>.lock. NEVER inside the repo (PRD §18.5 explicitly rejects .git/stagehand.lock). <hash> = sha256 hex of canonical absolute repo path (symlinks resolved via filepath.EvalSymlinks so two terminals in the same repo hash identically).
2. INPUT: A repo path string (from os.Getwd() in runDefault, default_action.go:48). No other inputs — this package is a self-contained leaf.
3. LOGIC:
   a. Add golang.org/x/sys to go.mod (go get golang.org/x/sys). Confirm it resolves; it is already a transitive dep of cobra/pflag on Linux but not in go.mod directly.
   b. Create internal/lock/lock.go with:
      - type Locker struct { file *os.File; path string } — holds the open fd and lock-file path.
      - func Acquire(repoPath string) (*Locker, error) — resolves lockDir(), computes sha256 hash of canonical repo path, opens <hash>.lock (O_CREATE|O_RDWR), calls the platform-specific flock (lock_unix.go). On EWOULDBLOCK → returns a typed *HeldError containing the holder's lock-file contents (pid/hostname/repo/snapshot) for the contention path. On success → writes initial contents (pid=os.Getpid(), hostname=os.Hostname(), repo=repoPath, timestamp=time.Now().UTC().Format(time.RFC3339), snapshot="") and sets the package-level singleton (var current atomic.Pointer[Locker]).
      - func (l *Locker) Release() — closes the fd (flock auto-releases); clears the singleton if it points to l. Idempotent.
      - func (l *Locker) SetSnapshot(sha string) — rewrites the lock file with the updated snapshot= field while holding the fd open. Uses the existing pid/hostname/repo/timestamp values.
      - func SetSnapshot(sha string) — package-level convenience: if current.Load() != nil, calls current.Load().SetSnapshot(sha). No-op if no lock held (library usable without the lock in tests).
      - func lockDir() (string, error) — XDG_RUNTIME_DIR (if abs) → XDG_CACHE_HOME (if abs) → ~/.cache/stagehand/locks. NO CWD fallback (a lock in the repo is the §18.5 anti-pattern — fail loud with an error).
      - type HeldError struct { Contents LockContents; Path string } — returned by Acquire when the lock is held by another process. LockContents has Pid, Hostname, Repo, Timestamp, Snapshot string fields, parsed from the holder's lock file.
      - func parseContents(data []byte) LockContents — bufio.Scanner + strings.SplitN(line, "=", 2).
   c. Create internal/lock/lock_unix.go (//go:build !windows): func flock(fd int) error using unix.Flock(fd, unix.LOCK_EX|unix.LOCK_NB). Map unix.EWOULDBLOCK to the HeldError.
   d. Create internal/lock/lock_windows.go (//go:build windows): no-op stub — flock always returns nil (lock always succeeds). The §13.5 CAS remains the guarantee on Windows. Add a comment documenting this rationale.
4. OUTPUT: A complete, self-contained internal/lock package importable by internal/cmd, internal/generate, and internal/decompose. Acquire(repoPath) returns (*Locker, error) or *HeldError. SetSnapshot(sha) is callable from the library layers. Release() is idempotent and auto-releases on process death.
5. MOCKING: No external services. Tests use temp directories and real flock (two goroutines/processes contending on the same lock file). For Windows-specific behavior, the build-tag split means lock_windows.go is never compiled on Linux CI.
6. DOCS: [Mode A] Update docs/configuration.md — add a subsection documenting lock-file location resolution via XDG_RUNTIME_DIR / XDG_CACHE_HOME / ~/.cache/stagehand/locks and the 'never inside the repo' rationale. Update docs/how-it-works.md — add a '### Per-repo run lock (FR52)' subsection under '## Safety and the rescue protocol' covering: the two-stage defense (lock + CAS), the per-host limit (shared/network filesystems are the CAS's job), the never-in-repo location rationale, the no-op fast path, and the flock auto-release property. Both ride WITH this work — do not create a separate docs subtask.

tasks.title: Contention behavior, Busy exit code, and wiring (both commit paths)
tasks.description: Add the Busy=5 exit code, the no-op fast path and busy refusal contention behaviors, and wire lock acquisition into runDefault (the single funnel for both single-commit and decompose paths). Wire lock.SetSnapshot calls into generate.go and decompose.go so the holder publishes its frozen tree SHA for the fast-path comparison. E2E-tested for the cross-process contention scenarios that unit tests cannot reach.

subtasks.title: Busy exit code in internal/exitcode
subtasks.context_scope:
CONTRACT DEFINITION:
1. RESEARCH NOTE: See integration_seams.md §2. The exit-code registry is at internal/exitcode/exitcode.go. Current constants (lines 22-30): Success=0, Error=1, NothingToCommit=2, Rescue=3, Timeout=124. For() at line 52 maps errors → codes via *ExitError short-circuit (no new errors.Is arm needed). ExitError at line 36: exitcode.New(code, err) lets a caller force a specific code; Err may be nil for a SILENT non-zero exit (the contention message is printed once by the caller, then exitcode.New(Busy, nil) prevents double-printing in main.go).
2. INPUT: None — this is a constant addition.
3. LOGIC: Add Busy = 5 to the const block (after Rescue=3, before Timeout=124 — keep Timeout=124 last as it mirrors GNU timeout). Value 5 is free (no collision with 0/1/2/3/124). No change to For() — Busy is only returned via explicit exitcode.New(exitcode.Busy, nil). Add a doc comment: 'Busy — another stagehand run holds the per-repo lock; retry after it finishes (FR52 §18.5)'.
4. OUTPUT: exitcode.Busy is a valid constant usable as exitcode.New(exitcode.Busy, nil) from runDefault. exitcode.For() returns 5 for that ExitError.
5. MOCKING: None.
6. DOCS: [Mode A] Update docs/cli.md ## Exit codes table (line ~366) — add a row: | 5 | Busy — another stagehand run holds the per-repo lock; retry after it finishes. |. Add a note below the table explaining the two contention behaviors (no-op exit 0 and busy exit 5) and that this is distinct from commit-failure codes so scripts can tell 'busy, retry' from 'failed'.

subtasks.title: Contention logic, acquire/release wiring in runDefault, and SetSnapshot calls in generate.go + decompose.go
subtasks.context_scope:
CONTRACT DEFINITION:
1. RESEARCH NOTE: See integration_seams.md §1, §3, §4. The lock acquires in runDefault (internal/cmd/default_action.go) after line 55 (g := git.New(repoDir)), before the auto-stage state machine (line 73). This single insertion covers both single-commit and decompose paths (runDecompose is called from runDefault). defer locker.Release() fires on every early-exit. The snapshot= field is published by the library layers via the lock.SetSnapshot() singleton.
2. INPUT: P1.M1.T1.S1 provides the internal/lock package (Acquire, Release, SetSnapshot, HeldError). P1.M1.T2.S1 provides exitcode.Busy. The existing git.Git interface provides WriteTree(ctx) (sha, error) — index-read-only, safe to call without the lock for the no-op fast-path comparison.
3. LOGIC:
   a. In internal/cmd/default_action.go, immediately after line 55 (g := git.New(repoDir)), add lock acquisition:
      - locker, lockErr := lock.Acquire(repoDir)
      - If lockErr is a *lock.HeldError: call a new helper handleLockContention(stderr, heldErr, g, ctx) that:
        (i) Reads the holder's snapshot= from heldErr.Contents.
        (ii) If snapshot is non-empty: take the contender's own WriteTree (g.WriteTree(ctx)) — index-read-only, safe without the lock. If the contender's tree SHA == holder's snapshot → nothing new staged → print 'nothing to do — an in-progress run already covers your staged changes.' to stderr and return exitcode.New(exitcode.Success, nil) (exit 0, SILENT — message already printed).
        (iii) Otherwise (snapshot empty, or tree SHAs differ): print the busy message to stderr: 'stagehand: another stagehand run is already in progress on <repo> (pid <N> on <host>). Your newly-staged changes will remain staged — re-run stagehand after it finishes. Lock: <path>.' and return exitcode.New(exitcode.Busy, nil) (SILENT — message already printed).
      - If lockErr is any other error: return exitcode.New(exitcode.Error, fmt.Errorf('acquire run lock: %w', lockErr)).
      - On success: defer locker.Release().
   b. In internal/generate/generate.go (~line 186, right after signal.SetSnapshot(treeSHA, parentSHA, "")): add lock.SetSnapshot(treeSHA). Import github.com/dustin/stagehand/internal/lock.
   c. In internal/decompose/decompose.go (~line 170, right after tStart is captured from FreezeWorkingTree): add lock.SetSnapshot(tStart). Import github.com/dustin/stagehand/internal/lock.
   d. Read-only subcommands bypass the lock naturally — they have their own RunE and never reach runDefault. No explicit bypass code is needed.
4. OUTPUT: Two concurrent stagehand invocations on one repo: the first acquires and proceeds normally; the second exits 0 (if nothing new staged) or 5 (Busy, naming the holder). The holder publishes snapshot= to enable the fast path. Single defer covers all exit paths.
5. MOCKING: Unit tests for handleLockContention use a mock git.Git (or the existing stubtest harness) returning a canned WriteTree SHA. The lock package itself is tested in P1.M1.T1.S1.
6. DOCS: [Mode A] Update docs/cli.md — add the contention-behavior note to the Busy exit code row's explanation (rides with P1.M1.T2.S1's cli.md update if not already done; coordinate). The how-it-works.md concurrency subsection was added in P1.M1.T1.S1 — cross-reference the no-op fast path there.

subtasks.title: E2E contention scenarios: held lock → Busy, accidental double-run → exit 0, no stale lock, dry-run/read-only bypass
subtasks.context_scope:
CONTRACT DEFINITION:
1. RESEARCH NOTE: See integration_seams.md §7. The e2e harness (internal/e2e/harness_test.go) compiles the binary once, creates temp repos (newRepo), invokes subprocesses (runStagehand), and has a deterministic concurrent-race primitive (waitForMarker, line 216-228). These are cross-process behaviors that unit tests cannot reach.
2. INPUT: P1.M1.T2.S2 provides the wired contention behavior. The e2e harness provides newRepo, runStagehand, waitForMarker, writeStubConfig, stubEnv.
3. LOGIC: Add e2e scenarios to internal/e2e/scenarios_test.go (or a new lock_scenarios_test.go file, matching the existing hook_scenarios_test.go pattern):
   a. **Busy refusal:** Create a repo, seed a commit, stage a change. Write a stub-agent config whose stub binary blocks on a marker file (sleeps until the marker appears — reuse the waitForMarker pattern). Launch stagehand subprocess #1 (blocking stub) in a goroutine. waitForMarker to confirm #1 holds the lock. Launch stagehand subprocess #2 against the same repo. Assert: #2 exits 5 (Busy) and stderr names the holder (pid, repo). Write the marker to release #1. Assert #1 commits successfully.
   b. **No-op fast path (accidental double-run):** Same setup. #1 holds the lock and has published snapshot= (it has snapshotted). #2 starts with nothing newly staged (same index). Assert: #2 exits 0 with 'nothing to do' message.
   c. **No stale lock:** After #1 exits (normally or via signal), verify #2 can immediately acquire the lock (flock auto-released). Assert: a fresh stagehand run proceeds without Busy.
   d. **Read-only bypass:** Run 'stagehand providers list', 'stagehand config path', 'stagehand models --help' against a repo where a lock is held. Assert: none exit Busy (they bypass runDefault entirely).
   e. **Dry-run acquires the lock:** Run 'stagehand --dry-run' while another run holds the lock. Assert: the dry-run exits Busy (dry-run goes through runDefault and calls WriteTree — it acquires the lock per FR52 scope).
4. OUTPUT: E2e test coverage for every contention scenario in the PRD §18.5 contention-behavior spec. All scenarios pass with go test -tags e2e.
5. MOCKING: The blocking stub agent is a shell script or Go binary that sleeps on a marker file — the same pattern the harness already uses (waitForMarker). No real agent is invoked.
6. DOCS: none — no user-facing/config/API surface change (this is test-only code).

tasks.title: Sync changeset-level documentation (Mode B)
tasks.description: Final cross-cutting documentation sweep: surface the race-free / safe-to-double-invoke safety property in README.md alongside the existing snapshot/atomic-commit pitch, and verify no stale concurrency claims survive anywhere in the docs tree.

subtasks.title: README.md race-free safety property + stale-claim sweep
subtasks.context_scope:
CONTRACT DEFINITION:
1. RESEARCH NOTE: See system_context.md §5 (defense in depth). README.md has an existing safety section covering the snapshot/atomic-commit pitch. The new property (race-free / safe-to-double-invoke) composes with it. This is a Mode B changeset-level doc — it only reads correctly once the whole feature lands, hence its dependency on all implementing subtasks.
2. INPUT: The implemented FR52 feature (lock + contention + Busy exit code) from P1.M1.T1.S1, P1.M1.T2.S2, P1.M1.T2.S3.
3. LOGIC:
   a. In README.md, find the safety section (alongside 'Never corrupt your repo' / the snapshot/atomic-commit pitch). Add a bullet or short paragraph: stagehand is safe to invoke twice — a per-repo run lock prevents two concurrent commit-producing runs from racing on HEAD, so an accidental double-run degrades gracefully (exit 0 'nothing to do' if nothing new is staged, or a clear 'busy, retry' exit otherwise). Keep it consistent with the per-host limit (don't overclaim shared-filesystem safety — the CAS covers that, not the lock).
   b. Grep the entire docs/ tree + README.md for stale concurrency claims: 'no lock', 'concurrent runs are safe without', or any language that implies two stagehand processes can safely race (they can't without the lock, and now they can't even try — the lock prevents it). Fix any stale wording.
   c. Verify docs/cli.md exit code table has the Busy=5 row (from P1.M1.T2.S1) and that docs/how-it-works.md has the Per-repo run lock subsection (from P1.M1.T1.S1). If either is missing, add it (they should ride with their implementing subtasks, but this is the catch-all).
4. OUTPUT: README.md and the docs/ tree are coherent with the FR52 feature — no stale claims, the new safety property is surfaced, the exit code and location docs are present.
5. MOCKING: None.
6. DOCS: This IS the documentation task (Mode B). No further doc work is needed after this subtask.
